title: Ceiba tomentosa
intro: Ceiba tomentosa, also known as Kapok or Velvet Tree, is a tropical tree species native to Central and South America, characterized by its unique, velvety leaves and long, straight trunk. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for centuries to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive problems, due to its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Ceiba tomentosa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Ceiba tomentosa is commonly prepared as a tea, tincture, or infusion, often in combination with other plants, to create remedies for various health conditions.
taxonomy: Ceiba tomentosa is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Malvaceae, commonly known as the silk floss tree. This plant is native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. It belongs to the genus Ceiba, which comprises approximately 30 species of deciduous trees. Ceiba tomentosa is often cultivated for its ornamental value and its ability to produce large, showy flowers and long, silky floss. The tree's classification is further supported by its unique characteristics, such as its broad, flat leaves and its massive, columnar trunk.
uses: Ceiba tomentosa, also known as Kapok, has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits. The plant's seeds and oil are said to possess anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, making them useful in treating skin conditions and wounds. In some cultures, the leaves and bark of Ceiba tomentosa are used to treat fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. Additionally, the plant's oil is also used as a natural remedy for skin conditions such as eczema and acne. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness and safety of these medicinal uses.

constituents: Ceiba tomentosa, also known as the Kapok tree, contains a variety of active constituents, including alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins. These compounds have been found to possess anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. The alkaloids present in Ceiba tomentosa, such as ceiba alkaloids, are known for their potential in treating various health conditions. The saponins in Ceiba tomentosa are believed to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, making it a potential natural remedy for various diseases.
parts: Ceiba tomentosa, the Kapok tree, has several medicinal parts that are utilized in traditional medicine. The leaves of Ceiba tomentosa are used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues. The bark of the tree is also used for its antiseptic properties to treat wounds and skin infections. The seeds of the Kapok tree have been traditionally used to treat diarrhea, dysentery, and other gastrointestinal issues. The roots of Ceiba tomentosa are used to treat fever, malaria, and other diseases caused by parasites.
preparations: Ceiba tomentosa is sometimes used in herbal preparations, although specific information on this is limited. Traditionally, this plant is considered medicinal in certain cultures. side_effects: Ceiba tomentosa, also known as Kapok tree, is native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. In some cultures, the plant and its seeds have been used in traditional medicine, but there is limited research on its safety and efficacy. However, some sources suggest that the plant may cause allergic reactions, gastrointestinal issues, and skin irritation in some individuals. Ceiba tomentosa seeds also contain a compound called urushiol, which can cause severe skin irritation and allergic reactions similar to those experienced with poison ivy. More research is needed to fully understand the potential side effects of Ceiba tomentosa.
